mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW 650i engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned 650i engine typically costs £3,500–£10,000 supply only depending on which generation (N62 or N63), with used units available from £2,000–£4,500. Fitting adds £2,000–£3,500. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your car.

It depends entirely on which generation. The N62 (2005–2010) has valve stem seal and cooling system issues but is otherwise robust. The early N63 (2011–2014) is notoriously unreliable — timing chain failures, excessive oil consumption, and turbocharger issues are common. Later N63TU (2014+) cars are significantly improved.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW 650i?

acceptedAnswer.text: N62: valve stem seal failure (excessive oil consumption, blue smoke), cooling system failures. N63: timing chain and guide failure, excessive oil consumption, turbocharger failure, valve stem seal leaks. The N63 is the more problematic of the two.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a BMW 650i engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value and generation. Against a well-kept F06 650i worth £8,000–£18,000, a £5,000–£9,000 recon is 30–60% — borderline but often worth it on a cherished example. Against a tired E63 650i with significant other issues, get a scrap valuation first.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW 650i engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. E63/E64 (2005–2010) uses N62B48B; F06/F12/F13 (2011–2018) uses N63B44A or N63B44B; G32 (2017–present) uses N63B44B / N63TU. Tell us your registration date and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the N63 timing chain, or does it need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: It needs replacement — the chain guides break and the tensioner wears. A full timing chain, tensioner, guide and VANOS sprocket replacement costs £2,500–£5,000. It's a labour-intensive job but far cheaper than a full engine replacement (£5,000+). The TU (Technical Update) versions are significantly improved. At the first sign of rattling from the front of the engine on an N63, book an inspection immediately.

hero.h1: BMW 650i Engine Replacement — Compare Prices & Get Quotes
hero.subHeadline: The 650i has worn three distinct V8 engines over its lifetime — the naturally aspirated N62, the twin-turbo N63, and the updated N63TU. Each has its own reliability profile, running costs, and common failures. Get quotes from UK specialists for reconditioned, rebuilt and used units today.

replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the current 650i market. N62-engined cars sit at the lower end; N63TU engines command a premium. Reconditioned N63 engines are significantly more expensive due to their complexity. See the Era Map above for generation-specific context.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a 650i V8 engine typically takes 14–20 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer). The N63's "hot-vee" design makes some repairs exceptionally labour-intensive.

problems.title: N62 Valve Stem Seal Failure
problems.symptoms: Blue smoke on cold start, excessive oil consumption (1L per 1,000 miles or more), puff of smoke when accelerating after idling
problems.repairCost: £2,500–£4,500 (valve stem seal replacement — requires cylinder head work)

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: valve stem seal failure on the N62 is a well-documented issue that leads to excessive oil consumption and potential catalytic converter damage

problems.recommendation: The N62's valve stem seals degrade over time, allowing oil to enter the combustion chamber. This is not a catastrophic failure — you can drive like that forever — but it leads to high oil consumption, blue smoke, and eventual catalytic converter failure. Replacement is expensive (£2,500–£4,500) because it requires cylinder head disassembly. Many owners choose to live with the oil consumption, but if you're buying an N62 650i, budget for this work or accept the oil top-ups. The N63 also suffers from valve stem seal leaks.

failureLink.label: Read the full valve stem seal failure guide

problems.title: N63 Timing Chain and Guide Failure
problems.symptoms: Rattling noise on cold start, metallic ticking from the front of the engine, engine warning light, cam timing errors, eventual chain slip
problems.repairCost: £2,500–£5,000 (timing chain, tensioner, guides and VANOS sprocket replacement)
problems.replacementCost: £5,000–£9,000 (if chain jumps and causes valve interference)

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: any rattling on an N63 requires immediate investigation

problems.recommendation: The N63's timing chain guides are made of brittle plastic that can break apart, sending metal fragments through the engine. The "hot-vee" design generates extreme heat loads that accelerate component wear. This is one of the most severe and common problems on the N63. BMW even launched a Customer Care Package program to address widespread issues. If you hear any rattling from the front of the engine on an N63 650i, book an inspection immediately. A preventative chain replacement is far cheaper than a full engine replacement.

failureLink.label: Read the full timing chain failure guide

problems.title: N63 Excessive Oil Consumption and Turbocharger Failure
problems.symptoms: Oil level dropping between services, blue smoke, low oil pressure warning, loss of power, whistling from engine bay, engine warning light
problems.recommendation: The N63 can burn up to a quart of oil in 1,000 miles. This is caused by valve stem seal leaks, turbocharger oil seal failure, and PCV system issues. The twin turbochargers are crammed into a tight engine bay, generating extreme heat. Turbocharger failures are well-documented. Check oil levels regularly — if you're topping up more than 1L between services, have the turbochargers and PCV system inspected. Ignoring oil consumption can lead to turbo failure and catastrophic engine damage.

failureLink.label: Read the full turbocharger failure guide

buyingChecks.title: Buying This 650i? Check These First
buyIf: * Post-2014 N63TU (Technical Update) — BMW addressed many of the early N63's reliability issues
* N62 with documented valve stem seal replacement — the defining issue on this engine
* Full service history with frequent oil changes — both engines are unforgiving of neglect
* Evidence of cooling system maintenance — especially important on the N62
avoidIf: * Early N63 (pre-2014) with no proof of timing chain work — you're buying a known risk
* Any rattling from the front of the engine on an N63 — timing chain issues are likely
* N62 with excessive oil consumption and no valve stem seal history — you'll be doing the work
* Poor service history — both engines are complex and expensive to repair
* Any engine warning light without a clear diagnosis

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The 650i is a genuinely desirable grand tourer — but the V8 engines that power it have well-documented weaknesses. The N62 (2005–2010) is the simpler of the two — naturally aspirated, but with valve stem seal and cooling system issues. The N63 (2011–present) is a twin-turbo masterpiece with a notorious reputation — early cars are high-risk; later TU cars are improved. Buy on history, maintain religiously, and the 650i will reward you with one of BMW's finest V8 experiences. Skip the history and it will punish you.

cta.label: → Compare 650i engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used BMW 650i (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: 1. Oil Smoke — Any blue smoke on cold start? (N62 valve stem seal warning)
2. Oil Consumption — How much oil does it use between services? (N63 turbo/valve seal warning)
3. Service History — including which generation (E63, F06/F12/F13, or G32) and evidence of major engine work
4. Cold Start Rattles (N63) — Listen for rattles lasting more than 1–2 seconds (timing chain tensioner warning)
5. Coolant Level — Any unexplained loss? (cooling system warning on both engines)
6. Turbo Performance (N63) — Test acceleration; any hesitation, whistling, or loss of power?
7. Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any unresolved issues?
8. MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures (catalyst, O2 sensors)
